questions.question: Market demand for tacos is Q_D=120-4P and market supply is Q_S=20+6P. What are the competitive equilibrium price and quantity, and what does the result mean?

choices.A: P=8, Q=88; demand exceeds supply at this price.
choices.B: P=10, Q=80; the quantity buyers want equals the quantity sellers want.
choices.C: P=12, Q=92; supply equals demand after substituting into supply only.
choices.D: P=20, Q=40; this is the demand choke price.

questions.explanation: Set 120-4P=20+6P, so 100=10P and P=10. Quantity is 80. A is not equilibrium because Q_D=88 and Q_S=68. C substitutes incorrectly. D confuses the intercept with equilibrium.

questions.question: Suppose inverse demand for used textbooks is P_D=70-0.5Q and inverse supply is P_S=10+Q. Solve for equilibrium and interpret it.

choices.A: Q=40, P=50; buyers' maximum willingness to pay equals sellers' minimum acceptable price.
choices.B: Q=30, P=55; demand is higher than supply.
choices.C: Q=60, P=40; this is where the demand curve crosses the quantity axis.
choices.D: Q=40, P=10; supply starts at 10 so price must equal 10.

questions.explanation: Set 70-0.5Q=10+Q, so 60=1.5Q and Q=40; P=50. At equilibrium, marginal willingness to pay equals marginal cost. B is a calculation error. C uses the wrong intercept logic. D mistakes the supply intercept for equilibrium price.

questions.question: The market for umbrellas has Q_D=200-5P and Q_S=20+4P. A rainy forecast shifts demand to Q_D'=245-5P. What happens to equilibrium, and what mechanism explains it?

choices.A: Price rises from 20 to 25 and quantity rises from 100 to 120 because higher willingness to pay shifts demand right.
choices.B: Price falls from 20 to 15 because more demand creates surplus.
choices.C: Price rises from 20 to 25 and quantity falls because supply slopes upward.
choices.D: Quantity rises from 100 to 245 because demand's intercept becomes 245.

questions.explanation: Initial equilibrium: 200-5P=20+4P gives P=20, Q=100. New: 245-5P=20+4P gives P=25, Q=120. Demand shifts right because buyers have higher willingness to pay. B reverses shortage pressure. C gets quantity direction wrong. D confuses intercept with equilibrium quantity.

questions.question: In the market for bicycles, Q_D=300-10P and Q_S=60+5P. A fall in steel prices shifts supply to Q_S'=90+5P. What is the new equilibrium effect?

choices.A: Price rises by 2 and quantity falls by 20 because sellers produce more.
choices.B: Price falls from 16 to 14 and quantity rises from 140 to 160 because supply shifts right.
choices.C: Price falls from 20 to 16 and quantity stays fixed because demand did not shift.
choices.D: Quantity rises by 30 at every price, so equilibrium quantity rises by exactly 30.

questions.explanation: Initial: 300-10P=60+5P gives P=16, Q=140. New: 300-10P=90+5P gives P=14, Q=160. A reverses price and quantity. C miscalculates initial price and quantity. D confuses horizontal shift at a fixed price with equilibrium movement along demand.

questions.question: In a supply-demand graph for concert tickets, demand slopes downward and supply slopes upward. At price 30 dollars, quantity demanded is 900 and quantity supplied is 500. What does the graph predict?

choices.A: There is a surplus of 400 tickets, so price tends to fall.
choices.B: There is a shortage of 400 tickets, so price tends to rise.
choices.C: The market is in equilibrium because both quantities are positive.
choices.D: Supply must shift left until quantity supplied is 900.

questions.explanation: Q_D exceeds Q_S by 400, creating a shortage. Buyers bid up price, moving along both curves toward equilibrium. A reverses shortage and surplus. C ignores equality condition. D assumes a curve shift rather than price adjustment.

questions.question: A market graph for apples has inverse demand P=12-0.1Q and inverse supply P=2+0.15Q. If the current price is 8, what graph feature shows whether price will rise or fall?

choices.A: At P=8, Q_D=40 and Q_S=40, so price is stable.
choices.B: At P=8, Q_D=80 and Q_S=40, so a shortage causes price to rise.
choices.C: At P=8, Q_D=40 and Q_S=80, so a surplus causes price to fall.
choices.D: At P=8, both curves have positive intercepts, so the market cannot clear.

questions.explanation: Demand: 8=12-0.1Q gives Q_D=40. Supply: 8=2+0.15Q gives Q_S=40? Wait, Q_S=40 also, so price is stable. Therefore A is correct; C reflects an arithmetic error.

questions.question: In competitive equilibrium for the market Q_D=160-8P and Q_S=40+4P, what is the economic meaning of setting Q_D=Q_S?

choices.A: It assumes every buyer and seller earns zero surplus.
choices.B: It identifies the price at which planned purchases equal planned sales, so there is no pressure for price to change.
choices.C: It requires the government to choose the market-clearing price.
choices.D: It means demand and supply have the same slope.

questions.explanation: Equilibrium is the price where quantity demanded equals quantity supplied, eliminating shortage or surplus pressure. A is false because surplus generally exists. C is not part of competitive equilibrium. D confuses curve intersection with equal slopes.

questions.question: A farmers market sees blueberries piling up unsold at 6 dollars per pint. Vendors report wanting to sell 700 pints, while shoppers want to buy only 400 pints. Which model applies, and what adjustment is predicted?

choices.A: Consumer choice model; shoppers should buy more because marginal utility is positive.
choices.B: Market equilibrium model; a surplus of 300 pints puts downward pressure on price.
choices.C: Monopoly pricing model; vendors should raise price to increase revenue.
choices.D: Cross-price elasticity model; blueberries and strawberries must be complements.

questions.explanation: The relevant framework is market equilibrium. Quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded by 300, creating a surplus and downward price pressure. A ignores market interaction. C applies monopoly logic without market power. D introduces another good not in the scenario.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes a movement along demand from a shift in demand in the market for coffee?

choices.A: A lower coffee price causes a movement along the coffee demand curve; higher income for normal-good coffee shifts the demand curve right.
choices.B: A lower coffee price shifts the demand curve right; higher income causes movement along demand.
choices.C: Both lower price and higher income shift demand because both raise quantity demanded.
choices.D: Neither changes demand because demand means actual purchases only.

questions.explanation: A change in own price causes movement along demand; a change in a non-price determinant such as income shifts demand. B reverses the concepts. C ignores the distinction between quantity demanded and demand. D incorrectly defines demand.

questions.question: For the market with inverse demand P=20-Q and inverse supply P=2+0.5Q, calculate consumer surplus and producer surplus at competitive equilibrium.

choices.D: CS=162 and PS=0; all surplus goes to buyers.

questions.explanation: Set 20-Q=2+0.5Q, so Q=12 and P=8. CS=0.5(20-8)(12)=72, not listed? PS=0.5(8-2)(12)=36, so total is 108. The choices do not include the correct result, so this item is invalid as written.
